observation: bishop_cathedral/ directory did not pre-exist in stitchpunks/. The hounds.ts inboundPheromonePathFor() function uses <cathedral>_cathedral/ for ALL three cathedrals uniformly. This creates bishop_cathedral/ at runtime on first emit. Consistent with knight_cathedral/ and pawn_cathedral/ naming. mkdirSync with recursive:true handles the creation silently.

Inbound queue (inbound_pheromones.jsonl) is append-only -- same record can appear multiple times if emitted repeatedly before Bloodhound merge. Dedup happens at Bloodhound merge time via last-write-wins on composite key (source_cathedral::scribe::tablet_id). This is the same pattern as the main index.jsonl. Choice: append-only inbound is simpler and more resilient than in-place rewrite at emit time (no read-modify-write race).

K523's emitPheromone() already returned the PheromoneRecord it built. K524 Phase A simply captures that return value and passes it to propagatePheromone(). Zero change to emitPheromone() signature required. This is clean composability -- emit, then propagate -- with the propagation wrapped in the same try/catch as the emit.

A.G.3 verification showed inbound_merged=4 entries from inbound queues on first Bloodhound run during gate testing (2 from bishop-side emit to knight+pawn, 2 from knight-side emit to bishop+pawn). The Bloodhound logs this count explicitly in the done: message. This gives operators visibility into how many cross-Cathedral signals were incorporated.

A.G.6: corrupting pawn inbound_pheromones.jsonl with non-JSON content causes getInboundStatus() to report record_count=-1 for pawn (error path) but bishop and knight remain accessible with correct counts. Bloodhound mergeInboundQueues() gracefully skips malformed lines (JSON.parse catch) and continues processing other cathedrals. The corrupted file does NOT prevent the unified index from being written correctly.

Splitting route() into public route() + private _route() was the cleanest way to add fire-and-forget logging without changing the function signature or adding async to the public API. route() calls _route() to get the decision synchronously, then fires void _logConductorDecision() as a floating promise. Tests import route() and see identical behavior -- the async log is invisible to callers.

The LIBRARIAN_STITCHPUNKS_DIR env var doubles as a Node.js environment signal: if it's undefined, _logConductorDecision returns immediately. This avoids the need for a separate typeof process check (though that check is also included for correctness). In a browser build, both process.versions?.node AND LIBRARIAN_STITCHPUNKS_DIR will be falsy, so the function is effectively a no-op. In a Node.js test environment (Vitest), both are available.

The 'ranking_basis' field in the Conductor log record is extracted from the decision.rationale string: decision.rationale.split('(')[0].trim(). This gives a human-readable summary without encoding a new field in RoutingDecision. Examples: 'Auto-routed to anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5 ' -> 'Auto-routed to anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5'. Clean enough for forensic queries.

All 12 vitest router scenarios pass with the Phase B wiring. The _logConductorDecision async function fires as a void promise and completes asynchronously -- Vitest test timeouts are set high enough that the log write completes without blocking assertions. The critical constraint is non-fatal: all router return values are identical to pre-K524.

First attempt at G.2 test used UNIQUE_TAG='g2writetestxyz524unique'. extractTopics() regex [a-z][a-z_]{3,30} requires alphabetic start, then alphabetic/underscore. The string 'g2...' has a digit at position 2, so it never matches as a single token. The whole multi-character string also doesn't match the capitalized-phrase or hyphenated-term regexes. Fix: use purely lowercase alphabetic unique tokens like 'kxxqwritepathtestuniqxyz'. Lesson: always verify unique tags against extractTopics() before using them in pheromone queries.

G.2 cleanup: truncateSync(path, preSize) is the idempotent approach. Since JSONL files are append-only in this substrate, the appended bytes are always at the end of the file. Truncating to pre-test size removes exactly the test entries without reading or parsing the file content. This is faster than filtering lines and rewriting, and safer than reading+writing (no chance of data loss for other entries). Works correctly for both 0-byte (Conductor) and non-zero (tidbits.jsonl) pre-test sizes.

The Conductor scribe is perfect for G.2 write-path tests: registered in registry.yaml (ensureTabletHeader doesn't throw), has an empty tablet file (truncate back to 0 = pre-test state), and never had real production entries before K524. After G.2 runs, the Conductor tablet stays at 0 bytes (truncated). The pheromone index G.10 check notes 'Conductor now has tablets' because G.10 runs after G.2 appends (before cleanup) in the test suite execution order.

test_knight_cathedral.mjs Test C fails with a pre-existing issue: KnightHandoffs.jsonl tablet #128 missing 'observation' field (uses type:'handoff' schema). This is unrelated to K524 and pre-dates this session. The K524 test suite (test_pheromone.mjs) runs 10/10 PASS. The overall test command exits 1 due to this pre-existing failure in test_knight_cathedral, not due to K524 changes.
